Curriculum → Networking, edge, and request routing → DNS fundamentals

Lesson 1 of 5

DNS fundamentals

DNS maps names to endpoints and lets you steer traffic geographically, by health, or by weighted rollout without changing clients.

25-35 min Networking, edge, and request routing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Many high-level designs start at the wrong place. DNS is often the first layer that shapes latency, failover, and migration strategy.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. What DNS actually does A and AAAA records map names to IPs2. Traffic steering patterns Use health checks to avoid routing users to failed regions3. Operational caveats Avoid depending on instant TTL expiry

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Many high-level designs start at the wrong place. DNS is often the first layer that shapes latency, failover, and migration strategy. 2. What DNS actually does A and AAAA records map names to IPs3. Traffic steering patterns Use health checks to avoid routing users to failed regions4. Operational caveats Avoid depending on instant TTL expiry
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Many high-level designs start at the wrong place. DNS is often the first layer that shapes latency, failover, and migration strategy.

What to say first

  • Explain recursive lookup, caching, and TTL.
  • Use DNS for coarse traffic steering, not every balancing decision.
  • Mention latency, geo, or weighted routing when multi-region matters.
2

Study section

What DNS actually does

Resolvers follow delegation from root to TLD to authoritative servers and cache answers based on TTL.

Call these points out explicitly

  • A and AAAA records map names to IPs
  • CNAME records alias names
  • Low TTLs help migrations but increase resolver traffic
3

Study section

Traffic steering patterns

Managed DNS can support weighted, latency-based, and geo-aware routing to shift users toward healthy regions or gradual rollouts.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use health checks to avoid routing users to failed regions
  • Remember that DNS failover is not instant because of caching
  • Steering works best when the application is region-aware
4

Study section

Operational caveats

DNS is eventually consistent and cached by many layers, so it is better for coarse routing than for per-request balancing.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Avoid depending on instant TTL expiry
  • Plan for stale records during incidents
  • Combine DNS with regional load balancers for fine-grained control

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

DNS resolution and delegation are useful to explain before regional routing decisions.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Explain recursive lookup, caching, and TTL.
  • Use DNS for coarse traffic steering, not every balancing decision.
  • Mention latency, geo, or weighted routing when multi-region matters.
  • Call out the delay between a DNS change and user impact.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Treating DNS as immediate failover.
  • Ignoring resolver and browser caching.
  • Putting application-level routing logic entirely in DNS.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • When would you use weighted DNS instead of an L7 load balancer?
  • How do TTL choices affect planned cutovers?
  • What happens if one region fails but clients still cache its record?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Content delivery networks Networking, edge, and request routingLoad balancing Networking, edge, and request routing

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: DNS fundamentals

Watch the edge, cache, and origin layers react as traffic shape changes across regions and protection controls. Apply it to dns fundamentals so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "DNS fundamentals" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around what dns actually does and watch how it interacts with traffic steering patterns.Many high-level designs start at the wrong place. DNS is often the first layer that shapes latency, failover, and migration strategy.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • CDN hit ratio
  • origin utilization
  • edge p95
  • policy saturation

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Signals

What the editor found

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Explain recursive lookup, caching, and TTL.
  • Use DNS for coarse traffic steering, not every balancing decision.
  • Mention latency, geo, or weighted routing when multi-region matters.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from What DNS actually does and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Explain recursive lookup, caching, and TTL. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from Traffic steering patterns.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "DNS fundamentals" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • When would you use weighted DNS instead of an L7 load balancer?
  • What DNS actually does
  • Traffic steering patterns
  • Explain recursive lookup, caching, and TTL.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
